title: Gynecomastia from Systemic, Endocrine & Nutritional Disorders: A Case-Based Atlas
subtitle: A Resident Training Module across organ failure (cirrhosis, CKD/ESRD), endocrine disease (Graves', acromegaly, GH therapy, feminizing ACC/Cushing) and iatrogenic causes (isoniazid, efavirenz) — the SHBG/estrogen:androgen logic, the must-not-miss feminizing tumour, and dechallenge/rechallenge causality.
authors: Resident training module — synthesis of 8 published cases (see sourceFile); original teaching text. All citations verified/corrected against PubMed/PMC.
sourceFile: user-provided compilation (Gynecomastia_Systemic_Etiologies_Resident_Training_Module.md), citations verified/corrected via PubMed/PMC: Gordon/Olivo/Rafii/Southren Ann Intern Med 1973;79(2):198–203 (cirrhosis); Schmitt/Shehadeh/Sawin Ann Intern Med 1968;69(1):73–79 (CKD/hemodialysis); Endocrinol Diabetes Metab Case Rep 2021 EDM20-0181 PMC8115411 (Graves'); Lim & Rao J Endocr Soc 2022;6(Suppl 1):A492 ENDO abstract ODP314 PMC9625425 (acromegaly); Acharya et al. Indian J Pediatr 2010;77:443–444 PMID 20140778 and Malozowski & Stadel J Pediatr 1995;126:659–661 PMID 7699552 (GH therapy); Umphonsathien et al. Kaohsiung J Med Sci 2018;34:705–706 and Alruwaili et al. Am J Case Rep 2022 PMC9801065 (feminizing ACC/Cushing & Carney complex); Khan & Agarwal Lung India 2012;29:189–191 PMC3354502 and Morrone et al. J Bras Pneumol 2008 (isoniazid); van Ramshorst et al. BMC Pediatr 2013;13:120 PMC3751361 (efavirenz). Refeeding gynecomastia is covered in cr-047 and not duplicated here.

patient.chiefComplaint: Gynecomastia as a window onto systemic, endocrine, or iatrogenic disease — a rise in the effective estrogen:androgen ratio at the breast
pmh: Organ failure — alcohol-related (Laennec's) cirrhosis; ESRD on maintenance haemodialysis
Endocrine — untreated Graves' disease; acromegaly (GH/PRL macroadenoma); recombinant GH therapy; feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma with ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome
medications: Disease-relevant agents and culprits: spironolactone (cirrhosis ascites), recombinant growth hormone, isoniazid, efavirenz, darbepoetin (CKD)

caseDescription: A case-based atlas of the systemic, endocrine and nutritional/iatrogenic causes of true gynecomastia — the ones that flag treatable, occasionally life-threatening, underlying disease. The breast acts as a bioassay of the estrogen-to-androgen balance, and each disorder tilts that balance by a recognisable mechanism: increased SHBG and impaired hepatic estrogen clearance (cirrhosis, thyrotoxicosis), uremic hypogonadism with hyperprolactinaemia (CKD), GH/IGF-1 and prolactin co-secretion (acromegaly), direct GH effects on prepubertal breast, autonomous tumoural aromatase with hypercortisolism (feminizing ACC), and drug-specific effects (isoniazid, efavirenz). The skill taught is pattern recognition: which systemic disease, by which mechanism, unlocked by which clue on history or labs.
keyFindings: Final common pathway: an increased effective estrogen:androgen ratio at the breast
SHBG binds testosterone ~5× more avidly than estradiol — anything that raises SHBG (thyrotoxicosis, cirrhosis) lowers FREE testosterone despite a 'normal' total
Always check TSH, LFTs, creatinine, IGF-1 and a drug history in new gynecomastia — systemic disease frequently presents this way
Very high estradiol with suppressed gonadotropins, rapid onset, weight change, or an abdominal mass = hunt for a feminizing tumour
Drug-induced gynecomastia (isoniazid, efavirenz, spironolactone) reverses on dechallenge; recurrence on rechallenge clinches causality
finalDiagnosis: Gynecomastia from systemic disease is a sign, not a diagnosis: management is cause-specific. Treat the organ failure (alcohol abstinence and liver care; dialysis/transplant for ESRD) and remove the avoidable contributor (switch spironolactone to eplerenone). Restore euthyroidism in Graves' and resect/medically control the pituitary tumour in acromegaly — both reverse the gynecomastia. Observe benign GH-therapy gynecomastia after excluding tumours and enzyme defects. Resect and stage a feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma (and screen a child with gynecomastia + Cushing + lentigines + a calcifying Sertoli tumour for Carney complex / PRKAR1A). For drug causes, dechallenge to a safe alternative (rifampicin/ethambutol-based regimen sparing isoniazid; dolutegravir/nevirapine for efavirenz) and recognise that early recognition preserves reversibility.
learningObjectives: Use SHBG and the free (not total) estrogen:androgen ratio to explain gynecomastia in cirrhosis and thyrotoxicosis.
Recognise uremic hypogonadism with hyperprolactinaemia and the refeeding-like onset of CKD/dialysis gynecomastia.
Diagnose acromegaly biochemically (IGF-1 + non-suppressed GH on OGTT) when gynecomastia is the presenting clue.
Identify the must-not-miss feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma and the Carney-complex phenotype in a child.
Apply dechallenge/rechallenge logic to isoniazid- and efavirenz-induced gynecomastia and distinguish true gynecomastia from antiretroviral lipodystrophy.

items.title: How systemic disease produces gynecomastia — the estrogen:androgen ratio as the final common pathway
items.content: True gynecomastia is proliferation of male glandular breast tissue, and every cause in this atlas works through one final common pathway: an increased effective estrogen-to-androgen ratio at the breast. That ratio rises three ways. (1) More estrogen — increased aromatisation (adipose, tumour), exogenous exposure, or impaired hepatic clearance. (2) Less androgen action — primary or secondary hypogonadism, a rise in SHBG that lowers free testosterone, or anti-androgen drugs. (3) A transient surge in the ratio — reactivation of the gonadal axis during refeeding (a 'second puberty'). For the systemic causes, the breast behaves as a bioassay of the estrogen:androgen balance, so the diagnostic skill is to ask which systemic disease tilts that balance, by what mechanism, and which clue on history or labs unlocks it.
keyPoints: Final common pathway: increased effective estrogen:androgen ratio at the breast
Three routes: more estrogen, less androgen action, or a transient ratio surge (refeeding)
The male breast is a bioassay of the estrogen:androgen balance

items.title: Why SHBG and hepatic estrogen clearance are the levers behind systemic gynecomastia
items.content: Two pieces of physiology explain most of this module. First, SHBG binds testosterone with roughly 5-fold higher affinity than estradiol, so anything that raises SHBG — thyrotoxicosis, cirrhosis — preferentially strips out free testosterone and raises the free estradiol-to-free testosterone ratio even when the total testosterone reads 'normal'. This is why a thyrotoxic or cirrhotic man can look hormonally normal on a total testosterone yet be functionally hypoandrogenised. Second, the liver clears estrogens and the precursor androstenedione, so cirrhosis both impairs estrogen clearance and increases peripheral aromatisation of androstenedione to estrogen. Add the kidney's role (CKD lowers prolactin clearance, producing hyperprolactinaemia and uremic hypogonadism) and the refeeding physiology (malnutrition suppresses gonadotropins; repletion restarts the axis), and the systemic patterns fall into place. The practical corollary: order a free (or calculated free) testosterone and an SHBG, not just a total testosterone, when systemic disease is in play.
keyPoints: SHBG binds testosterone ~5× more avidly than estradiol — high SHBG lowers FREE testosterone
Cirrhosis impairs estrogen clearance and increases androstenedione aromatisation
Measure free/calculated-free testosterone + SHBG, not just total testosterone

items.title: Why a cirrhotic man feminises — the uniformly raised estradiol:testosterone ratio (Case 1)
items.content: The landmark study of 13 cirrhotic men (Gordon GG, Olivo J, Rafii F, Southren AL, Ann Intern Med 1973;79:198–203) defined the hormonal milieu: low total and free testosterone, low dihydrotestosterone, high total and free estradiol, elevated LH with normal FSH, and — the single unifying abnormality present in ALL patients — a supranormal estradiol:testosterone ratio. The mechanism combines a raised SHBG (which lowers free testosterone) with increased extraglandular aromatisation of androstenedione whose hepatic clearance is impaired; the precise drivers remain debated (paradoxically, giving testosterone to cirrhotics raises estradiol yet reduces gynecomastia prevalence). The clinical trap is to blame the liver alone: spironolactone, used for ascites, independently causes painful gynecomastia (it antagonises the androgen receptor, displaces estradiol from SHBG and inhibits 17β-HSD), so the first move in a cirrhotic who develops NEW painful gynecomastia is to switch spironolactone to eplerenone. Severity tracks with estrogen excess (especially estrone) and higher Child–Pugh/MELD class.
keyPoints: Signature: ↑ E2:T ratio (present in all), ↑ SHBG, ↑ LH, normal FSH, liver stigmata
Mechanism: ↑ SHBG lowers free T + impaired hepatic estrogen clearance and ↑ androstenedione aromatisation
Pitfall: spironolactone causes painful gynecomastia — switch to eplerenone before blaming the liver alone

items.title: How chronic kidney disease and dialysis cause gynecomastia — uremic hypogonadism, hyperprolactinaemia and a refeeding effect (Case 2)
items.content: A man on maintenance haemodialysis for ESRD develops bilateral retroareolar gynecomastia with impaired sexual function and small soft testes. Three mechanisms combine. (1) Uremic hypogonadism — uremia directly damages the testis, lowering testosterone (often with raised LH/FSH). (2) Hyperprolactinaemia — reduced renal clearance of prolactin (a stalk/sellar 'disconnection' component can add to it); a reported case had prolactin 4285 mU/L that fell to 481 on bromocriptine. (3) A refeeding-like effect — effective dialysis reverses the malnourished catabolic state and reactivates the gonadal axis, exactly the physiology of the classic series of transient gynecomastia in haemodialysis patients (Schmitt GW, Shehadeh I, Sawin CT, Ann Intern Med 1968;69:73–79, where 6 of 7 patients were affected). Onset is typically 2–9 months after starting dialysis. Because CKD patients take many gynecomastia-inducing drugs (calcium-channel blockers, PPIs, ACE inhibitors, spironolactone, H2 blockers, and reversibly darbepoetin), scrutinise the medication list before invoking uremia alone. Renal transplantation most reliably corrects both the hormonal and nutritional drivers.
keyPoints: Three drivers: uremic hypogonadism + hyperprolactinaemia (↓ renal clearance) + refeeding-like reactivation on dialysis
Onset 2–9 months after starting dialysis (classic Schmitt 1968 series)
Audit the heavy CKD medication list; transplantation is the most reliable cure

items.title: Why every new gynecomastia needs a TSH — thyrotoxicosis raises SHBG and lowers free testosterone (Case 3)
items.content: A 49-year-old man presented with right-sided gynecomastia; directed questioning then revealed thyrotoxic symptoms, and work-up showed a suppressed TSH, high free T4, positive TSH-receptor antibody (Graves'), a markedly elevated SHBG (143 nmol/L; normal 18–54), a normal total testosterone but a LOW calculated free testosterone (0.12 nmol/L), and a normal estradiol, prolactin and hCG (Endocrinol Diabetes Metab Case Rep 2021, EDM20-0181; PMC8115411). Thyroid hormone does two things: it stimulates peripheral aromatase (raising estrogen) and increases hepatic SHBG synthesis, which lowers free testosterone while the total stays normal — so the free E2:free T ratio climbs. Gynecomastia is found in up to ~40% of thyrotoxic men but is rarely the presenting complaint, which is exactly why a TSH belongs in every gynecomastia panel even when the patient looks well. Restoring euthyroidism (thionamide ± later levothyroxine, or radioiodine) normalised SHBG and free testosterone and resolved the gynecomastia within ~3 months, with no recurrence at 2 years. Unilateral presentation occurs and can mimic a breast mass — image if eccentric or hard.
keyPoints: Signature: ↓ TSH, ↑ FT4, markedly ↑ SHBG, LOW calculated free testosterone, normal E2
Mechanism: thyroid hormone ↑ aromatase and ↑ SHBG → ↑ free E2:free T
Check TSH in every gynecomastia work-up; euthyroidism resolves it in ~3 months

items.title: How gynecomastia can be the presenting clue to acromegaly (Case 4)
items.content: A 50-year-old man with metabolic syndrome, carpal tunnel syndrome and obstructive sleep apnoea was referred for 5 months of bilateral gynecomastia with fatigue and erectile dysfunction, and denied the classic acral changes (Lim F & Rao A, 'Gynecomastia as the Initial Presentation of Acromegaly', J Endocr Soc 2022;6(Suppl 1):A492, ENDO 2022 abstract ODP314; PMC9625425). Labs showed hyperprolactinaemia (49.3 ng/mL), low testosterone (186 ng/dL) with inappropriately normal-low LH/FSH (secondary hypogonadism), a high IGF-1 (585 ng/mL) and non-suppressed GH after oral glucose, with a 2.3 cm pituitary macroadenoma on MRI. A somatotroph (often GH/prolactin co-secreting — PRL is co-secreted in ~25% of acromegaly) macroadenoma drives the picture: hyperprolactinaemia and tumoural compression cause secondary hypogonadism (low testosterone → relative estrogen effect), and GH/IGF-1 may act on breast tissue directly. The diagnosis is biochemical — IGF-1 screens and a non-suppressed GH on OGTT confirms; MRI cannot distinguish a functioning from a non-functioning tumour. Definitive therapy (transsphenoidal surgery ± somatostatin analogue/pegvisomant) normalised prolactin, IGF-1 and testosterone and completely resolved the gynecomastia. The teaching constellation: gynecomastia + carpal tunnel + OSA + pre-diabetes should prompt an IGF-1.
keyPoints: Confirm acromegaly biochemically: high IGF-1 + non-suppressed GH on OGTT (MRI cannot prove function)
Gynecomastia is driven by hyperprolactinaemia/secondary hypogonadism ± direct GH/IGF-1 effect
Constellation clue: gynecomastia + carpal tunnel + OSA + pre-diabetes → check IGF-1

items.title: Why prepubertal gynecomastia on growth-hormone therapy is benign — but only after tumours are excluded (Case 5)
items.content: A prepubertal boy on recombinant GH for short stature develops subareolar breast tissue about 6 months after starting, with otherwise prepubertal genitalia and axis — gynecomastia dissociated from true puberty (Acharya SV, Gopal RA, Bandgar TR, Menon PS, Shah NS, Indian J Pediatr 2010;77:443–444; PMID 20140778). The foundational FDA series (Malozowski S & Stadel BV, J Pediatr 1995;126:659–661; PMID 7699552) collected 22 such cases with onset 0.5–7 months after GH start; GH/IGF-1 is thought to sensitise or stimulate breast glandular tissue and modulate local aromatase/steroidogenesis. The condition is benign and self-limited — in the index case it resolved spontaneously without stopping GH, and in the FDA series outcomes varied (some resolved on continued GH, some after stopping). The essential caveat is that prepubertal gynecomastia is never normal: before attributing it to GH you must exclude an adrenal adenoma/carcinoma, a testicular tumour (including large-cell calcifying Sertoli cell tumour), 11β-hydroxylase deficiency and familial aromatase excess. Once those are excluded, management is reassurance and observation; GH usually need not be discontinued.
keyPoints: New prepubertal gynecomastia within ~0.5–7 months of starting GH, no other pubertal signs
Benign and self-limited — GH usually need not be stopped
But prepubertal gynecomastia is never normal: exclude adrenal/testicular tumour, 11β-OHase deficiency, aromatase excess first

items.subtopic: Bucket B — Endocrine: feminizing ACC / Cushing
items.title: Why painful gynecomastia with Cushingoid features and an abdominal mass means a feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma until proven otherwise (Case 6)
items.content: A 43-year-old man had 4 months of gynecomastia, low libido and weight gain, with bilateral gynecomastia, testicular atrophy, a Cushingoid habitus and a palpable abdominal mass; work-up showed a high 24-hour urinary free cortisol with a LOW ACTH (ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome), a markedly elevated estradiol (1221 pmol/L), and a 12×13×15 cm left adrenal mass with lung metastases (Umphonsathien et al., 'The coexistence of Cushing syndrome and gynecomastia as the manifestations of adrenocortical carcinoma', Kaohsiung J Med Sci 2018;34:705–706). A feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma secretes both cortisol (ACTH-independent Cushing) and estrogen — the latter from increased tumoural aromatase and peripheral conversion of tumour-derived weak androgens; feminizing tumours are only ~2–6% of ACCs and most are malignant. Resection (with mitotane for advanced disease) drops estradiol and regresses the gynecomastia, but prognosis is poor. A crucial paediatric variant: a child with gynecomastia + Cushing syndrome + lentigines + a large-cell calcifying Sertoli cell tumour should be screened for Carney complex (PRKAR1A) — exactly the phenotype reported in a 14-year-old (Alruwaili et al., Am J Case Rep 2022; PMC9801065). The rule: very high estradiol with ACTH-independent hypercortisolism and an adrenal mass demands adrenal imaging and complete hormonal profiling before surgery.
keyPoints: Feminizing ACC: very high estradiol + ACTH-independent hypercortisolism + a large adrenal mass (mostly malignant)
Image the adrenals and profile hormones fully before surgery; mitotane for advanced disease
Child + gynecomastia + Cushing + lentigines + calcifying Sertoli tumour → Carney complex (PRKAR1A)

items.title: How dechallenge and rechallenge prove isoniazid-induced gynecomastia (Case 7)
items.content: A 45-year-old man on antitubercular therapy (rifampicin + isoniazid + pyrazinamide) for tubercular lymphadenitis developed a painful, enlarging left breast mound (~4 cm of true glandular tissue) after about 3 months, with a normal hormone panel, normal liver/renal/thyroid function and ultrasound confirming glandular hypertrophy (Khan AA & Agarwal R, 'Isoniazid related gynecomastia: description of a case and systematic review', Lung India 2012;29:189–191; PMC3354502). The mechanism is uncertain — proposed contributors are isoniazid-induced disturbance of pyridoxine (vitamin B6) metabolism altering the androgen–estrogen balance, and a refeeding-type physiology as the wasting illness of TB recovers; classic onset is ≥3 months into therapy. The causality logic is the teaching point: WHO-UMC 'probable' from the temporal link and dechallenge response, and — most powerfully — Morrone et al. (J Bras Pneumol 2008;34:978–981) documented recurrence on rechallenge 8 years later, an 'experiment of nature' that clinches isoniazid as the cause. Management spares the TB cure: reassurance, increasing pyridoxine, and regimen modification (rifampicin/ethambutol-based, sparing isoniazid) rather than surgery, since it resolves over months once isoniazid is withdrawn.
keyPoints: Painful true gynecomastia ≥3 months into ATT with a normal hormone panel
Causality: dechallenge response + recurrence on rechallenge (Morrone) is the strongest evidence
Don't compromise TB cure — pyridoxine + regimen modification; it resolves, so avoid surgery

items.title: Why efavirenz gynecomastia must be separated from antiretroviral lipodystrophy (Case 8)
items.content: Efavirenz is the antiretroviral most frequently implicated in true gynecomastia (mechanism incompletely defined; possible estrogenic/anti-androgenic effects). A 7-year-old girl developed true gynecomastia just 4 months after starting abacavir/lamivudine/efavirenz (van Ramshorst MS, Kekana M, Struthers HE, McIntyre JA, Peters RPH, BMC Pediatr 2013;13:120; PMC3751361); replacing efavirenz with nevirapine halved the breast size at 2 weeks and resolved it by ~6 weeks. By contrast, an adult man whose efavirenz gynecomastia went unaddressed for years progressed to grade III and required bilateral mastectomy with marked psychological morbidity. The central management point is to distinguish true gynecomastia (firm retroareolar glandular tissue, confirmable on breast ultrasound) from the pseudogynecomastia of NRTI-related lipodystrophy (fat redistribution, not glandular tissue) — mislabelling lipodystrophy as gynecomastia leads to inappropriate drug switching, while genuine efavirenz gynecomastia is reversible if caught early (switch to dolutegravir or nevirapine once other causes are excluded). The contrast between the rapidly reversible paediatric case and the surgical adult case is the lesson: early recognition preserves reversibility.
keyPoints: Efavirenz is the ART agent most implicated in TRUE gynecomastia
Distinguish true gynecomastia (glandular, US-confirmed) from NRTI lipodystrophy (fat) before switching drugs
Reversible if caught early (switch to dolutegravir/nevirapine); late cases need surgery

items.title: How to work up gynecomastia for a systemic cause — history, the targeted panel, then imaging
items.content: Start by confirming true gynecomastia (firm, concentric, mobile subareolar tissue) and screening for male breast cancer: a hard, eccentric, fixed mass or bloody nipple discharge triggers mammography/ultrasound first. Then a history and drug review (spironolactone, efavirenz, isoniazid, GH, darbepoetin, anti-androgens) — a clear drug-temporal link warrants dechallenge with expected resolution. If no culprit drug, send the targeted panel: TSH/free T4, LFTs, creatinine/eGFR, total and free (or calculated free) testosterone, LH/FSH, estradiol, prolactin, β-hCG and IGF-1. The pattern points the way: suppressed TSH with high FT4 and high SHBG = thyrotoxicosis (Graves'); deranged LFTs with cirrhosis stigmata = hepatic; low eGFR with uremic hypogonadism ± high prolactin = CKD; very high estradiol with ACTH-independent hypercortisolism and an adrenal mass = feminizing ACC; high IGF-1 with non-suppressed GH = acromegaly; and an all-normal panel after recent nutritional recovery = refeeding (reassure; resolves in 1–2 years). A very high estradiol with suppressed gonadotropins, rapid onset, weight loss or an abdominal mass mandates adrenal/gonadal imaging for a feminizing tumour (and, in a child with lentigines and a calcifying Sertoli tumour, screening for Carney complex).
keyPoints: Confirm glandular tissue + exclude breast cancer → history/drug review → targeted panel → imaging
Panel: TSH/FT4, LFTs, Cr/eGFR, total+free testosterone, LH/FSH, E2, prolactin, β-hCG, IGF-1
Very high E2 + suppressed gonadotropins/rapid onset/abdominal mass → image for a feminizing tumour

items.title: The eight systemic causes at a glance
items.content: Organ failure. Cirrhosis (Case 1): liver stigmata, ↑E2:T, ↑SHBG, ↑LH — treat the liver, switch spironolactone→eplerenone for painful gynecomastia. CKD/ESRD (Case 2): uremic hypogonadism ± hyperprolactinaemia, onset 2–9 months after dialysis starts — audit drugs; transplant cures best. Endocrine. Graves' (Case 3): ↓TSH, ↑FT4, markedly ↑SHBG, low free testosterone — restore euthyroidism, resolves in ~3 months. Acromegaly (Case 4): ↑IGF-1, non-suppressed GH on OGTT, ↑prolactin — biochemical diagnosis; surgery reverses it. GH therapy (Case 5): new prepubertal gynecomastia within months of GH, benign/self-limited — but exclude tumours/CAH. Feminizing ACC/Cushing (Case 6): very high estradiol + ACTH-independent cortisol + adrenal mass — image adrenals; child + lentigines → Carney complex (PRKAR1A). Iatrogenic. Isoniazid (Case 7): painful gynecomastia ≥3 months into ATT, normal hormones — resolves on withdrawal; rechallenge proves cause. Efavirenz (Case 8): true gynecomastia on EFV (not lipodystrophy) — reversible if caught early; switch EFV.
keyPoints: Organ failure: cirrhosis (↑E2:T, spironolactone trap) and CKD (uremic hypogonadism + prolactin)
Endocrine: Graves' (SHBG), acromegaly (IGF-1/OGTT), GH therapy (benign), feminizing ACC (image adrenals)
Iatrogenic: isoniazid and efavirenz — dechallenge resolves; distinguish efavirenz GM from lipodystrophy

items.title: Module appraisal — strengths, limitations and citation corrections
items.content: Strength: organising the systemic causes by the estrogen:androgen-ratio mechanism gives a reproducible work-up (TSH, LFTs, eGFR, IGF-1, drug history) and reliably surfaces the must-not-miss feminizing tumour. Citation note: every case was checked against PubMed/PMC and several attributions in the source compilation were corrected to the verified records — the acromegaly abstract is by Lim & Rao (not 'Khan'); the feminizing-ACC/Cushing case is Umphonsathien et al., Kaohsiung J Med Sci 2018;34:705–706 (not 'Lin C-J', and the pages differ from the source); and the Carney-complex case is Alruwaili et al. (not 'Alotaibi'). Limitations to flag as doubt: two anchors are conference abstracts (acromegaly, J Endocr Soc/ENDO 2022; and the modern CKD case), which establish the presentation but not incidence — the CKD bucket is therefore anchored on the classic Schmitt 1968 series. The exact lab values are illustrative across assays and eras (the cirrhosis and CKD primary data are from the 1960s–70s), so apply your own laboratory's reference ranges. Refeeding gynecomastia is deliberately NOT duplicated here (it is covered in cr-047). Management statements are educational, not prescriptive; several decisions (mitotane staging, drug substitution) are specialist-individualised.
keyPoints: Citations verified/corrected: acromegaly = Lim & Rao; feminizing ACC = Umphonsathien et al. (Kaohsiung 2018;34:705–706); Carney = Alruwaili et al.
Two conference-abstract anchors (acromegaly, modern CKD); CKD bucket leans on the classic Schmitt 1968 series
Numbers are illustrative across eras/assays; refeeding is covered in cr-047 (not duplicated); statements are educational

items.question: Why can a thyrotoxic or cirrhotic man have a normal TOTAL testosterone yet obvious feminization?
items.answer: Because gynecomastia tracks the FREE estrogen-to-androgen ratio at the breast, and a high SHBG decouples free testosterone from total testosterone. SHBG binds testosterone with about 5-fold greater affinity than estradiol, so when SHBG rises — as it does in thyrotoxicosis (thyroid hormone increases hepatic SHBG synthesis) and in cirrhosis — it preferentially sequesters testosterone, lowering the free (bioavailable) fraction while the total testosterone, which includes the SHBG-bound pool, can still look normal. The free estradiol is comparatively spared, so the free E2:free T ratio climbs and the breast feminises. In the Graves' case the total testosterone was normal but the calculated free testosterone was frankly low (0.12 nmol/L) with an SHBG of 143 nmol/L. The practical lesson: measure SHBG and a free or calculated-free testosterone — a normal total testosterone does not exclude functional hypoandrogenism.
keyPoints: Gynecomastia tracks the FREE E2:T ratio, not total testosterone
High SHBG (thyrotoxicosis, cirrhosis) lowers free testosterone while total stays normal
Always order SHBG + free/calculated-free testosterone in systemic gynecomastia

items.question: How do you establish that a drug (e.g. isoniazid) caused a patient's gynecomastia?
items.answer: Build a causality case the way pharmacovigilance does. First, a temporal relationship: the gynecomastia begins a plausible interval after starting the drug (classically ≥3 months for isoniazid) and there is no better competing explanation once a hormone panel and organ-function and tumour screen are normal. Second, dechallenge: stopping (or substituting) the drug is followed by regression over weeks to months — for isoniazid, switching to a rifampicin/ethambutol-based regimen and the swelling subsides. Third, and most decisive, rechallenge: if re-exposure reproduces the gynecomastia, causation is effectively proven — Morrone et al. documented recurrence when isoniazid was given again 8 years later. Formal scales (WHO-UMC, Naranjo) codify this into 'possible/probable/definite'. The clinical corollary is that you rarely need surgery for drug-induced gynecomastia caught early — dechallenge to a safe alternative, and reserve intervention for fibrotic, long-standing tissue.
keyPoints: Temporal link + no better explanation (normal hormones/organ/tumour screen)
Dechallenge → regression; rechallenge → recurrence is the strongest proof (Morrone, isoniazid)
Early dechallenge usually avoids surgery; use WHO-UMC/Naranjo to grade causality

items.stem: A man with alcohol-related cirrhosis on spironolactone for ascites develops new, painful bilateral gynecomastia. What is the best first step?
options: Reassure only and take no action
items.explanation: Spironolactone is a classic cause of painful gynecomastia (androgen-receptor antagonism, displacement of estradiol from SHBG, 17β-HSD inhibition). Switching to eplerenone — a selective mineralocorticoid antagonist with negligible androgen-receptor affinity — relieves it while maintaining ascites control. Testosterone and surgery are inappropriate first moves, and simply reassuring ignores a reversible culprit.

items.stem: In the landmark series of cirrhotic men with feminization, which laboratory abnormality was UNIFORMLY present?
items.explanation: Gordon et al. (Ann Intern Med 1973) found low testosterone and DHT, high estradiol, elevated LH and normal FSH, but the single unifying abnormality present in all patients was a supranormal estradiol:testosterone ratio. SHBG is raised (not low) in cirrhosis, LH is elevated, and FSH is normal.

items.stem: A previously malnourished man on maintenance haemodialysis develops bilateral tender gynecomastia 3 months after starting dialysis. Which mechanism best explains the timing?
options: A refeeding-like reactivation of the gonadal axis as the catabolic state reverses
items.explanation: Effective dialysis reverses the malnourished, catabolic uremic state and reactivates the gonadal axis, producing a transient estrogen:androgen imbalance like refeeding (and like puberty) — the classic Schmitt 1968 observation, with onset typically 2–9 months after starting dialysis. The other options are not explained by the dialysis timing.

items.stem: A thyrotoxic man with gynecomastia has a NORMAL total testosterone but a LOW calculated free testosterone. The best explanation is:
items.explanation: Thyroid hormone increases hepatic SHBG synthesis; because SHBG binds testosterone far more avidly than estradiol, the free testosterone falls while the total (which includes the bound pool) stays normal, raising the free E2:free T ratio. Aromatase is stimulated (not deficient) in thyrotoxicosis, and the picture is not explained by low LH or a prolactinoma.

items.stem: A 50-year-old man with new gynecomastia, hyperprolactinaemia, carpal tunnel syndrome and obstructive sleep apnoea is suspected of acromegaly. Which test CONFIRMS the diagnosis?
options: Failure of GH to suppress after an oral glucose load (OGTT)
items.explanation: Acromegaly is confirmed biochemically: IGF-1 screens, and a non-suppressed GH after oral glucose (OGTT) confirms autonomous GH secretion. A random GH is too variable; MRI demonstrates a tumour but cannot prove it is functioning; and prolactin reflects co-secretion, not GH excess.

items.stem: A prepubertal boy develops gynecomastia 6 months after starting growth hormone for short stature, with otherwise prepubertal genitalia. What is the most appropriate approach?
options: Exclude adrenal/testicular tumours and enzyme defects, then reassure and observe
Immediately stop GH and obtain a breast biopsy
Diagnose normal puberty and take no action
items.explanation: GH-therapy gynecomastia is benign and usually self-limited (GH often need not be stopped), BUT prepubertal gynecomastia is never normal — adrenal adenoma/carcinoma, testicular tumour (including LCCSCT), 11β-hydroxylase deficiency and familial aromatase excess must be excluded before attributing it to GH. Biopsy, empirical tamoxifen, or calling it normal puberty are all inappropriate.

items.stem: A 43-year-old man has gynecomastia, Cushingoid features, a markedly elevated estradiol, a low ACTH, and a large adrenal mass. The most likely diagnosis is:
items.explanation: Very high estradiol with ACTH-independent hypercortisolism (low ACTH) and a large adrenal mass is a feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma until proven otherwise — it co-secretes cortisol and estrogen (tumoural aromatase). Klinefelter and refeeding do not produce a feminizing adrenal mass, and a macroprolactinoma is a pituitary, not adrenal, lesion.

items.stem: A 14-year-old boy has gynecomastia, Cushing syndrome, skin lentigines, and a large-cell calcifying Sertoli cell tumour. Which condition should be screened for?
items.explanation: Gynecomastia + Cushing syndrome + spotty pigmentation (lentigines) + a large-cell calcifying Sertoli cell tumour is the Carney complex phenotype, caused by PRKAR1A mutations — pursue genetic testing. MEN1, MEN2/RET and FGFR3 do not fit this constellation.

items.stem: A man on antitubercular therapy develops painful gynecomastia at 3 months. What finding would most strongly establish isoniazid as the cause?
options: Recurrence of gynecomastia on rechallenge with isoniazid
items.explanation: Recurrence on rechallenge is the strongest causality evidence (documented by Morrone et al. when isoniazid was re-given years later). A normal hormone panel, bilaterality, and a 3-month onset are consistent with the diagnosis but do not, by themselves, prove drug causation.

items.stem: An HIV-positive patient on efavirenz-based ART develops breast enlargement. What is the most important distinction before changing therapy?
options: True glandular gynecomastia versus NRTI-related lipodystrophy (pseudogynecomastia)
items.explanation: Efavirenz causes true gynecomastia, whereas NRTIs cause fat redistribution (lipodystrophy / pseudogynecomastia). A breast ultrasound showing retroareolar glandular tissue confirms true gynecomastia and justifies switching efavirenz; mislabelling lipodystrophy as gynecomastia leads to inappropriate drug changes. Pain, laterality and CD4 count do not drive that decision.

items.statement: A TSH should be checked in every new gynecomastia work-up, even when the patient appears clinically well.
items.explanation: True. Gynecomastia occurs in up to ~40% of thyrotoxic men and can be the presenting complaint before other thyrotoxic features are obvious, so a TSH belongs in the routine gynecomastia panel — thyrotoxicosis is an easily missed, readily treatable cause.

items.statement: Prepubertal gynecomastia appearing during growth-hormone therapy can be attributed to GH without any further work-up because it is benign.
items.explanation: False. Although GH-therapy gynecomastia is usually benign and self-limited, prepubertal gynecomastia is never normal: an adrenal or testicular tumour, 11β-hydroxylase deficiency and familial aromatase excess must be excluded before attributing it to GH.

items.statement: Chronic kidney disease can cause hyperprolactinaemia independent of any pituitary adenoma.
items.explanation: True. The kidney clears prolactin, so reduced renal clearance in CKD raises prolactin even without a pituitary tumour (a stalk/sellar 'disconnection' component can add to it), contributing to uremic hypogonadism and gynecomastia.

items.assertion: A thyrotoxic man with gynecomastia may have a low free testosterone despite a normal total testosterone.
items.reason: Thyroid hormone increases hepatic SHBG synthesis, and SHBG binds testosterone more avidly than estradiol.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. A higher SHBG preferentially sequesters testosterone, lowering the free fraction while the total (bound + free) stays normal, raising the free estradiol-to-free testosterone ratio. Hence option 0.

items.assertion: A pituitary MRI showing a macroadenoma is sufficient to confirm acromegaly in a man with gynecomastia.
items.reason: MRI reliably distinguishes a growth-hormone-secreting tumour from a non-functioning adenoma.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Acromegaly is confirmed biochemically (high IGF-1 with non-suppressed GH on OGTT); MRI demonstrates a tumour but cannot distinguish a functioning from a non-functioning adenoma, so imaging alone does not confirm the diagnosis. Both false = option 4.

items.question: List the core targeted laboratory panel for a man with new gynecomastia in whom a systemic cause is suspected.
items.answer: TSH and free T4; liver function tests; creatinine/eGFR; total and free (or calculated free) testosterone with SHBG; LH and FSH; estradiol; prolactin; β-hCG; and IGF-1. This panel separates thyrotoxic (↓TSH/↑FT4/↑SHBG), hepatic (abnormal LFTs), renal (low eGFR ± high prolactin), feminizing-tumour (very high estradiol ± suppressed gonadotropins → image), and acromegalic (high IGF-1 → confirm with OGTT-GH) causes, with a drug history running alongside.

items.question: Which clinical triad should make you image the adrenals urgently in a man with gynecomastia, and what is the unifying diagnosis?
items.answer: A markedly elevated estradiol + ACTH-independent hypercortisolism (Cushingoid features with a low ACTH) + a palpable abdominal/adrenal mass. The unifying diagnosis is a feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma (co-secreting cortisol and estrogen), which is usually malignant — image the adrenals and complete the hormonal profile before surgery.

items.question: Why must true gynecomastia be distinguished from lipodystrophy in a patient on antiretroviral therapy, and how?
items.answer: Because they have different culprits and management: efavirenz causes true glandular gynecomastia (reversible by switching efavirenz, e.g. to dolutegravir/nevirapine), whereas NRTIs cause lipodystrophy/pseudogynecomastia (fat redistribution), and mislabelling the latter leads to inappropriate drug switching. Distinguish them by palpation and breast ultrasound — true gynecomastia shows firm retroareolar glandular tissue, lipodystrophy shows fat without a glandular disc.
